skill.name: screen-reader-testing
skill.description: Guide AI coding agents through practical screen reader testing for web applications, including VoiceOver, NVDA, JAWS, TalkBack, and common ARIA patterns.
skill.summary: Validate screen reader behavior, keyboard navigation, ARIA usage, forms, dialogs, live regions, and dynamic content.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, crypto, key, and reconnaissance patterns, but semantic review found they are markdown examples, keyboard shortcut text, ARIA attributes, and accessibility terminology. The skill contains no executable scripts, no automated network requests, no credential access, and no prompt injection attempts. The only URLs are user-facing documentation links, so the skill is safe to publish.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer treated many fenced examples and shortcut lists as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The referenced content is documentation for screen reader setup, keyboard commands, checklists, HTML examples, and JavaScript examples. It is not executable skill code and does not run external commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are all markdown guidance or fenced examples. No script file, runtime hook, command invocation, or user-input command construction exists in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Accessibility Terms Flagged as Sensitive Keys or Weak Crypto
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged terms such as keyboard key handling, ARIA attributes, JAWS, and shortcut names as certificate files or weak cryptography. In context, these lines describe accessibility testing behavior and UI keyboard events, not cryptographic operations or secret material.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line context confirms accessibility documentation and JavaScript keyboard event examples. No certificate, private key, hashing, encryption, or secret file handling is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Documentation Links Flagged as Network Risk
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are public references for VoiceOver, NVDA, JAWS, and WebAIM documentation. They are markdown links for readers and do not perform background network access or transmit data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in a Resources section as markdown links. No code fetches these URLs, sends user data, or depends on remote execution.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Critical Combination Heuristic Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The critical heuristic combined unrelated static signals from documentation examples. Review found no credential access, no automated network exfiltration, no obfuscation, and no executable command path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The entire skill consists of markdown instructions and examples. The dangerous combination is not semantically present after reviewing both files.

content.value_statement: Screen reader issues are hard to find with visual checks alone. This skill provides structured test steps, checklists, and examples for assistive technology validation.
actual_capabilities: Explains testing workflows for VoiceOver, NVDA, JAWS, TalkBack, and Narrator.
Provides screen reader command references for common navigation and form tasks.
Supplies checklists for page load, headings, landmarks, forms, links, buttons, tables, and dynamic content.
Shows accessible patterns for modals, live regions, progress updates, tabs, and form errors.
Helps debug common ARIA and focus management issues in web applications.
Guides manual validation across desktop and mobile assistive technology combinations.
limitations: It does not automate screen reader software or install assistive technology tools.
It does not replace testing with real users who rely on assistive technology.
It focuses on web applications, not native desktop or mobile application audits.
It provides examples and checklists, but final WCAG conformance still requires expert review.

use_cases.title: Pre-release Accessibility Review
use_cases.description: Run structured screen reader checks before shipping a new page, form, modal, or interactive widget.

use_cases.title: Assistive Technology Bug Triage
use_cases.description: Reproduce reported screen reader issues and document expected announcements, focus behavior, and keyboard results.

use_cases.title: Design System Component Validation
use_cases.description: Validate reusable components such as dialogs, tabs, buttons, alerts, and live regions across screen reader combinations.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Test Checklist
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner screen reader testing for a single page
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a screen reader testing checklist for this web page. Include headings, landmarks, links, buttons, forms, errors, and dynamic content.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Cross-Screen Reader Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan screen reader test coverage for a production web app. Prioritize VoiceOver, NVDA, JAWS, TalkBack, browsers, platforms, and manual test steps.

prompt_templates.title: Debug an Accessibility Failure
prompt_templates.scenario: Investigate a specific screen reader bug
prompt_templates.prompt: Help debug this screen reader issue. Describe likely causes, manual reproduction steps, expected announcements, and fixes for ARIA or focus behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an Interactive Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this interactive component for screen reader support. Check role, name, state, focus order, keyboard behavior, live announcements, and edge cases.

output_examples.input: A checkout form needs screen reader validation before release.
output: A prioritized checklist covering labels, required fields, error messages, focus movement, instructions, and successful submission announcements.
Recommended coverage for NVDA with Firefox and VoiceOver with Safari.
Expected notes for each form control, including role, accessible name, state, and error association.

output_examples.input: A modal dialog is not announced correctly by screen readers.
output: A triage plan that checks dialog role, modal state, title association, description association, initial focus, trapped focus, and Escape handling.
Likely fixes for missing labels, unfocused headings, incorrect background access, and missing focus restoration.

output_examples.input: A team needs guidance for testing dynamic search results.
output: A manual test script for triggering results, checking polite announcements, validating loading states, and confirming focus remains predictable.
Guidance on when to use status, alert, progressbar, and log patterns.

best_practices: Test with real screen readers on supported platform and browser combinations.
Start with semantic HTML, then add ARIA only when native elements cannot express the behavior.
Document expected announcements, focus movement, keyboard behavior, and observed differences by screen reader.
anti_patterns: Do not rely only on automated accessibility scanners for screen reader compatibility.
Do not test only the happy path; include error states, loading states, and dynamic updates.
Do not assume one screen reader represents all assistive technology behavior.

faq.question: Does this skill run screen reader software automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides manual testing workflows, command references, checklists, and accessibility implementation examples.

faq.question: Which screen readers does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers VoiceOver, NVDA, JAWS, TalkBack, and Narrator, with practical browser and platform guidance.

faq.question: Can it replace WCAG expert review?
faq.answer: No. It helps structure screen reader validation, but formal conformance decisions need accessibility expertise.

faq.question: Is this useful for developers using Claude, Codex, or Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. It gives AI coding agents concrete checks and examples for accessible web implementation work.

faq.question: What kinds of components are covered?
faq.answer: It covers forms, buttons, links, headings, landmarks, tables, dialogs, live regions, progress updates, and tabs.

faq.question: Does it require network access or credentials?
faq.answer: No. The skill is documentation-based and only includes public reference links for users to open if needed.
